content.text: A connector is the bridge between a source system (Confluence, SharePoint, Slack, Jira, GitHub) and your RAG ingestion pipeline. It authenticates against the source, discovers documents changed since the last run, retrieves their content and permissions, and hands normalized records to the chunker-embedder. Getting connectors right is mostly an engineering problem — rate limits, pagination, token refresh, and ACL mapping — not an AI problem. This topic covers the canonical patterns so you can build reliable connectors for any enterprise source.

attrs.alt: Enterprise RAG connector architecture: source systems → connector layer (BaseConnector, incremental sync, rate limiting, webhooks) → normalizer (NormalizedDoc, HTML→Markdown, ACL extraction) → ingestion pipeline (chunk, embed, upsert, deletion, observability). Bottom section compares polling, webhook, and hybrid patterns.

content.text: Define a protocol that every source connector must satisfy. This lets the ingestion pipeline treat all sources uniformly: call
to fetch the full body, and
to get its permission groups. The output is a
— a source-agnostic record the pipeline can chunk and embed without knowing where the data came from.

Most enterprise APIs use OAuth 2.0 with short-lived access tokens and longer-lived refresh tokens. Connectors must transparently refresh the token before it expires — an expired token mid-sync should trigger a refresh and retry, not a crash. Store the refresh token encrypted at rest (e.g., in AWS Secrets Manager or a KMS-encrypted column), never in plain environment variables in production.
Never re-index everything on every run. Store a
cursor per connector in a database. On each run, call the source's
filter to only retrieve documents that changed. Most APIs paginate responses — follow
(SharePoint) until the cursor is absent. Respect rate limits with exponential backoff on HTTP 429.

Polling has a lag proportional to the poll interval. For documents where freshness matters (e.g., incident runbooks, policy pages), combine polling with webhooks. The webhook receiver validates the source's HMAC signature, then enqueues a

task for the affected document. Crucially, do not process the document inside the webhook handler — the HTTP response must return within a few seconds or the source will retry, causing duplicate processing.

import hashlib
import hmac

from fastapi import FastAPI, Header, HTTPException, Request

app = FastAPI()
WEBHOOK_SECRET = b"your-confluence-webhook-secret"


async def enqueue_sync(doc_id: str, event_type: str) -> None:
    # Push to your task queue (Celery, ARQ, SQS, etc.)
    # The worker calls connector.get_content(doc_id) and re-indexes
    ...


@app.post("/webhooks/confluence")
async def confluence_webhook(
    request: Request,
    x_hub_signature: str = Header(None),
):
    body = await request.body()

    # Validate HMAC-SHA256 signature
    if x_hub_signature:
        expected = "sha256=" + hmac.new(
            WEBHOOK_SECRET, body, hashlib.sha256
        ).hexdigest()
        if not hmac.compare_digest(expected, x_hub_signature):
            raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Bad signature")

    payload = await request.json()
    event = payload.get("eventType", "")
    doc_id = payload.get("page", {}).get("id")

    if not doc_id:
        return {"status": "ignored"}  # non-page event

    if event in ("page_created", "page_updated"):
        await enqueue_sync(doc_id, event)
    elif event == "page_deleted":
        await enqueue_sync(doc_id, "delete")

    return {"status": "queued"}

Different sources serialize content differently: Confluence uses Confluence Storage Format (XML/HTML), SharePoint returns HTML, Slack yields JSON message arrays, GitHub returns Markdown directly. The normalizer must convert all of these to clean Markdown before chunking. Use
for HTML conversion; preserve code blocks and tables which carry semantic meaning. Strip navigation chrome, ads, and repeated boilerplate that would pollute chunks with noise.

import re

from markdownify import markdownify as md


def _html_to_markdown(html: str) -> str:
    # Convert, then clean up common Confluence storage format noise
    text = md(html, heading_style="ATX", bullets="-", code_language=True)

    # Remove empty lines left by Confluence macros that markdownify can't convert
    text = re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", text)

    # Strip Confluence "info" / "note" macro wrappers (already visible as quotes)
    text = re.sub(r"^\s*\[!NOTE\]\s*", "> ", text, flags=re.MULTILINE)

    return text.strip()


def normalize_slack_thread(messages: list[dict], channel_name: str) -> NormalizedDoc:
    """Flatten a Slack thread into a single NormalizedDoc."""
    lines = []
    for msg in messages:
        ts = msg.get("ts", "")
        user = msg.get("user", "unknown")
        text = msg.get("text", "")
        lines.append(f"**{user}** ({ts}): {text}")

    thread_id = messages[0]["ts"]
    return NormalizedDoc(
        doc_id=f"slack::{channel_name}::{thread_id}",
        title=f"#{channel_name} thread {thread_id}",
        text="\n\n".join(lines),
        url=f"https://slack.com/archives/{channel_name}/p{thread_id.replace('.', '')}",
        source="slack",
        author=messages[0].get("user", ""),
        updated_at=datetime.fromtimestamp(float(thread_id), tz=timezone.utc),
    )

In production you run connectors for multiple sources. A registry pattern gives you a single entry point: iterate registered connectors, run each in its own error boundary so one failing connector does not block others, and persist cursors per-connector after a successful run.
filtering, some sources surface unchanged documents (e.g., a page touched by a label change). Computing a

of the extracted Markdown text and storing it alongside the doc's vector record lets the pipeline skip chunking and embedding if the hash is unchanged — cutting embedding API costs significantly for large corpora.

import hashlib


async def upsert_document(doc: NormalizedDoc, db_pool, embed_fn) -> bool:
    """Returns True if the document was re-indexed, False if unchanged."""
    text_hash = hashlib.sha256(doc.text.encode()).hexdigest()

    # Check stored hash from the last index run
    row = await db_pool.fetchrow(
        "SELECT content_hash FROM doc_index WHERE doc_id = $1", doc.doc_id
    )
    if row and row["content_hash"] == text_hash:
        return False  # skip: content identical

    # Content changed — re-chunk, re-embed, upsert
    chunks = semantic_split(doc.text, max_tokens=400)
    for i, chunk_text in enumerate(chunks):
        chunk_id = f"{doc.doc_id}::{i}"
        embedding = await embed_fn(chunk_text)
        await db_pool.execute(
            """
            INSERT INTO chunks (chunk_id, doc_id, text, embedding, acl_groups, acl_users, acl_public)
            VALUES ($1,$2,$3,$4,$5,$6,$7)
            ON CONFLICT (chunk_id) DO UPDATE SET
                text=EXCLUDED.text, embedding=EXCLUDED.embedding,
                acl_groups=EXCLUDED.acl_groups, acl_users=EXCLUDED.acl_users,
                acl_public=EXCLUDED.acl_public
            """,
            chunk_id, doc.doc_id, chunk_text, embedding,
            doc.acl_groups, doc.acl_users, doc.acl_public,
        )

    await db_pool.execute(
        """
        INSERT INTO doc_index (doc_id, content_hash, updated_at)
        VALUES ($1,$2,$3)
        ON CONFLICT (doc_id) DO UPDATE SET
            content_hash=EXCLUDED.content_hash, updated_at=EXCLUDED.updated_at
        """,
        doc.doc_id, text_hash, doc.updated_at,
    )
    return True

Deleted documents are a common source of stale retrieval. The ingestion pipeline must track which chunk IDs belong to each document, then remove all of them on a delete event. A tombstone table provides an audit trail and prevents re-indexing a deleted document if a delayed event arrives out of order.

from datetime import datetime, timezone


async def delete_document(doc_id: str, db_pool) -> int:
    """Remove all chunks for doc_id; returns count deleted."""
    # Delete all embedding rows for this document
    result = await db_pool.execute(
        "DELETE FROM chunks WHERE doc_id = $1", doc_id
    )
    count = int(result.split()[-1])  # "DELETE N"

    # Remove from doc_index
    await db_pool.execute(
        "DELETE FROM doc_index WHERE doc_id = $1", doc_id
    )

    # Write tombstone for audit + idempotency
    await db_pool.execute(
        """
        INSERT INTO tombstones (doc_id, deleted_at)
        VALUES ($1, $2)
        ON CONFLICT (doc_id) DO UPDATE SET deleted_at=EXCLUDED.deleted_at
        """,
        doc_id, datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc),
    )
    return count


async def is_tombstoned(doc_id: str, db_pool) -> bool:
    row = await db_pool.fetchrow(
        "SELECT 1 FROM tombstones WHERE doc_id = $1", doc_id
    )
    return row is not None
A connector failure that goes unnoticed silently ages the index. Instrument every run with metrics and structured logs:
— counter per connector per run. Alert when a run completes with 0 for an unusually long period.
. Alert when lag exceeds 2× the polling interval.
— labelled by error type (auth, rate_limit, parse). Distinguish transient from permanent failures.
cursor after a fully clean run. On failure, the next run re-processes from the last successful cursor so no documents are skipped.
— documents that fail to index after N retries go to a DLQ for manual inspection. Log the
, connector, and exception to speed up diagnosis.

For production, use a hybrid approach: webhooks for near-real-time updates plus a polling fallback every 15–60 minutes to catch missed events. The polling run also handles the initial full sync and cursor recovery after downtime. Always store the cursor
the sync window starts (not after it ends) so that documents modified while the sync is running are not skipped by the next run.
